vulnerability.title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
vulnerability.description: Determine where the audit logs are stored with the following command: $ sudo grep -iw log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf log_file = /var/log/audit/audit.log Configure the audit log to be protected from unauthorized read access by setting the correct permissive mode with the following command: $ sudo chmod 0600 audit_log_file By default, audit_log_file is "/var/log/audit/audit.log".
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q audit && rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then if LC_ALL=C grep -iw ^log_file /etc/audit/auditd.conf; then FILE=$(awk -F "=" '/^log_file/ {print $2}' /etc/audit/auditd.conf | tr -d ' ') else FILE="/var/log/audit/audit.log" fi chmod 0600 $FILE else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The rule mandates setting file permissions on audit logs to 0640 or less permissive, which can be safely corrected with a chmod command. No authentication or system-wide stability is at risk, and the change is low‑impact.

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for openscap_079 (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_file_permissions_var_log_audit). Could not verify.

review_feedback: Remediation script failed to apply the required permissions; chmod and touch commands returned exit code 255, leaving the vulnerability unresolved. Suggestions: Verify file existence before applying chmod; create it with proper permissions if missing.; Set permissions to 0600 as required by the rule, not 0640.; Simplify script: single chmod command after determining the audit log path.; Add proper error handling and exit on failure.; Ensure script runs with sufficient privileges (e.g., root) and validate auditd service state after change.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Remediation script failed to apply the required permissions; chmod and touch commands returned exit code 255, leaving the vulnerability unresolved.
concerns: Exit code 255 indicates command failure; likely due to missing file or insufficient privileges.
Uses chmod 0640 instead of the mandated 0600 or less permissive.
Unnecessary touch command adds complexity and can fail.
Script does not handle missing audit.log gracefully.
Redundant chmod commands and lack of error handling reduce reliability.
suggested_improvements: Verify file existence before applying chmod; create it with proper permissions if missing.
Set permissions to 0600 as required by the rule, not 0640.
Simplify script: single chmod command after determining the audit log path.
Add proper error handling and exit on failure.
Ensure script runs with sufficient privileges (e.g., root) and validate auditd service state after change.
